paper:867751779943579657.en_title: Diffusion quantum Monte Carlo calculation of the quasiparticle effective mass of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas

paper:867763861497839690.authors: N. D. Drummond | R. J. Needs
paper:867763861497839690.en_title: Quantum Monte Carlo calculation of the energy band and quasiparticle effective mass of the two-dimensional Fermi fluid
paper:867763861497839690.zh_title: 二维费米流体能带和准粒子有效质量的量子蒙特卡罗计算

variables.content: The occupied single-particle energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ of the interacting two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) was computed using the diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) method by taking total-energy differences on adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$; the resulting discrete DMC band values for the occupied states were described by a least-squares fit to the quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$, where $\alpha_{0}$, $\alpha_{2}$, and $\alpha_{4}$ are fitted coefficients.  These DMC calculations were performed for both the paramagnetic (unpolarized) and the ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEGs at density-parameter values $r_{s}=1$, $5$, and $10$ (atomic units, where lengths are given in Bohr radii).  The fitted quartic shows clear nonquadratic behavior: for the paramagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}>0$ at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, for the ferromagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}<0$ at all three densities studied, and for the paramagnetic fluid at $r_{s}=1$ the fitted DMC band is very close to the free-electron dispersion $\mathcal{E}(k)=k^{2}/2$ (all energies in Hartree atomic units).

Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

For the interacting 2D HEG energy-band data sets produced by DMC at the densities ($r_s=1,5,10$) and system sizes reported, truncating the band representation to a quartic polynomial in $k$, $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$, captures the essential curvature: the least-squares fits yield residuals that are small compared with the quoted uncertainties, so that the fitted coefficients $\alpha_2$ and $\alpha_4$ and derived quantities (occupied bandwidth and derivative at $k_F$ used to obtain $m^{*}$) are accurate within the stated error bars and higher-order terms ($k^6$ and beyond) are negligible over the occupied $k$-range used for the reported extractions.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

The fitted DMC bands deviate from a purely quadratic dependence on $k$: when the fitted quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ is used, the quartic coefficient $\alpha_4$ is positive for the paramagnetic HEG at $r_s=5$ and $10$ a.u., while $\alpha_4$ is negative for the ferromagnetic HEG at all densities studied; because these fitted bands are non-quadratic, thermodynamic properties that depend on integrals over the dispersion (for example temperature-dependent contributions to the free energy and specific heat at elevated temperatures) are predicted to depart from the free-particle-like behavior that would follow from a strictly quadratic dispersion $\propto k^2$.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

The DMC-calculated quasiparticle effective mass exhibits opposite trends with density for the two spin polarizations studied: in the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ increases with increasing $r_{s}$ (decreasing density), producing a significant enhancement at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, whereas in the fully ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ decreases as density is lowered; these calculated trends are quantitatively consistent with the experimental observations of Tan et al. for paramagnetic systems and of Padmanabhan et al. for ferromagnetic or partially spin-polarized systems.
